title: The Brothers Karamazov
author: Dostoyevsky, Fyodor
summary: Fyodor Pavlovitch Karamazov is a senseless, debauched landowner whose reckless appetites and neglect shape a troubled family. His first marriage to Adelaïda Ivanovna Miüsov ends in elopement, scandal, and the widow’s dying in Petersburg, while their eldest son Dmitri (Mitya) is abandoned and raised by the faithful servant Grigory. Fyodor Pavlovitch squanders alliances, introduces a harem, and revels in buffoonery; his second marriage produces Ivan and Alexey (Alyosha). The household’s decay, social gossip, and political echoes—through cousins like Pyotr Miüsov returning from Paris—begin a saga of moral conflict and looming tragedy. The narrative previews complicated loyalties, self-deception, and religious questioning.

characters.name: Fyodor Pavlovitch Karamazov
characters.personality: senseless, fantastical, selfish, debauched, buffoonish, greedy
characters.lifespan: Wastes life in debauchery; remembered after a gloomy, tragic death (to be described later)

characters.name: Alexey Fyodorovitch Karamazov (Alyosha)
characters.personality: gentle, devoutly presented as the third son; described with sympathy
characters.lifespan: Alive during narrated events; third son whose role is introduced early

characters.name: Dmitri Karamazov (Mitya)
characters.age: about 3 years old at abandonment; later a young man
characters.personality: eldest son; abandoned as a child; impulsive and central to family scandal
characters.lifespan: Left in servant care as a child (raised by Grigory); fate to be further developed

characters.name: Ivan Karamazov
characters.personality: intelligent, aloof (introduced as a son by second marriage)
characters.lifespan: Alive during narrated events; future role left to be detailed

characters.name: Adelaïda Ivanovna Miüsov
characters.lifespan: Elopes with a divinity student and later dies in Petersburg (garret; typhus or starvation)

characters.name: Grigory
characters.lifespan: Takes charge of and raises Mitya as an infant; remains the loyal family servant

characters.name: Pyotr Alexandrovitch Miüsov
characters.age: young man when returning from Paris; older later
characters.personality: enlightened, Europeanized, a Liberal with continental contacts
characters.lifespan: Returns from Paris; mentioned as having known Proudhon and Bakunin; political life sketched

characters.name: Mitya's grandmother (unnamed)
characters.personality: widow, seriously ill, moved to Moscow
characters.lifespan: Living but seriously ill and removed to Moscow

characters.name: Divinity student (unnamed)
characters.lifespan: Runs away with Adelaïda to Petersburg; implicated in her destitution and death

characters.name: Adelaïda's daughters (unnamed)
characters.personality: members of her family; married off
characters.lifespan: Mentioned as married and thus not caring for Mitya initially

characters.name: Proudhon
characters.personality: political thinker referenced as acquaintance of Pyotr
characters.lifespan: Referenced only as known to Pyotr Alexandrovitch

characters.name: Bakunin
characters.personality: revolutionary thinker referenced as acquaintance of Pyotr
characters.lifespan: Referenced only as known to Pyotr Alexandrovitch

unusual_words.definition: owner of land; a country estate holder
unusual_words.context: Describes Fyodor Pavlovitch as a 'land owner well known in our district.'

unusual_words.definition: dark, somber, or mournful
unusual_words.context: Used to describe the tragic death remembered among the district.

unusual_words.definition: causing or characterized by extreme distress or sorrow
unusual_words.context: Refers to Fyodor Pavlovitch's 'gloomy and tragic death.'

unusual_words.definition: lacking common sense or judgment; irrational
unusual_words.context: Describes Fyodor Pavlovitch as a 'senseless, fantastical fellow.'

unusual_words.definition: extremely bad or severe; wretched
unusual_words.context: Used in the phrase 'a type abject and vicious.'

unusual_words.definition: deliberately cruel or violent; morally bad
unusual_words.context: Paired with 'abject' to portray the family's patriarchal type.

unusual_words.definition: the quality of being without sense or reason
unusual_words.context: Explains the father's peculiar character as 'senselessness.'

unusual_words.definition: strange or unusual
unusual_words.context: Refers to a 'peculiar national form' of senselessness.

unusual_words.definition: imaginary or fanciful; odd in character
unusual_words.context: Describes certain 'fantastical fellows' in the district.

unusual_words.definition: a sycophant; one who flatters for personal gain
unusual_words.context: Says Fyodor 'fastened on them as a toady.'

unusual_words.definition: astute or sharp in practical matters
unusual_words.context: Contrasts 'shrewd and intelligent' with mere senselessness.

unusual_words.definition: having or showing intelligence; clever
unusual_words.context: Used to contrast many 'fantastical fellows' who are still intelligent.

unusual_words.definition: solid or firm; here part of 'hard cash'
unusual_words.context: Appears in 'a hundred thousand roubles in hard cash.'

unusual_words.definition: money in coins or notes
unusual_words.context: Part of 'hard cash' indicating money kept by Fyodor.

unusual_words.definition: relating to luxury or sensual pleasure
unusual_words.context: Describes Fyodor's 'voluptuous temper.'

unusual_words.definition: a woman's undergarment; used metaphorically for women
unusual_words.context: Says he was 'ready to run after any petticoat.'

unusual_words.definition: easily influenced or persuaded
unusual_words.context: Refers to a 'pliable imagination' persuading the bride.

unusual_words.definition: the faculty of forming new ideas or images
unusual_words.context: Describes how imagination led to Adelaïda's decision.

unusual_words.definition: showing a willingness to take risks; brave or confident
unusual_words.context: Used in 'bold and ironical spirits' to characterize the era.

unusual_words.definition: characterized by irony or sardonic humor
unusual_words.context: Says Fyodor was thought once to be 'ironical.'

unusual_words.definition: living at the expense of another; exploitative
unusual_words.context: Describes Fyodor's 'parasitic position' socially and economically.

unusual_words.definition: the quality of being pleasantly stimulating or exciting
unusual_words.context: Asked 'What gave the marriage piquancy' referring to the elopement.

unusual_words.definition: an act of running away to get married secretly
unusual_words.context: The marriage was 'preceded by an elopement.'

unusual_words.definition: a woman who inherits or is entitled to inherit wealth
unusual_words.context: Adelaïda is described as an 'heiress.'

unusual_words.definition: strong, healthy, and full of energy
unusual_words.context: Describes Adelaïda as a 'vigorous, intelligent girl.'

unusual_words.definition: characterized by idealized or adventurous notions
unusual_words.context: Refers to the 'romantic generation' who made dramatic choices.

unusual_words.definition: mysterious or difficult to understand
unusual_words.context: Describes an 'enigmatic passion' in the narrator's anecdote.

unusual_words.definition: strong emotion or enthusiasm
unusual_words.context: Used in 'enigmatic passion' to explain behavior.

unusual_words.definition: impossible to overcome
unusual_words.context: Refers to 'insuperable obstacles' invented to prevent marriage.

unusual_words.definition: things that block one's way or impede progress
unusual_words.context: Appears with 'insuperable obstacles' in the anecdote.

unusual_words.definition: a very steep or overhanging place; cliff
unusual_words.context: Used in the suicide anecdote: 'threw herself from a high bank, almost a precipice.'

unusual_words.definition: visually attractive, like a picture
unusual_words.context: Says her chosen spot was 'picturesque,' which influenced her action.

unusual_words.definition: commonplace or unromantic
unusual_words.context: Contrasts 'picturesque' with a 'prosaic flat bank' that might have prevented the suicide.

unusual_words.definition: the act of intentionally causing one's own death
unusual_words.context: Used in the anecdote about the young lady who perished.

unusual_words.definition: lacking experience or sophistication; innocent
unusual_words.context: Says 'people, even the wicked, are much more naïve and simple-hearted than we suppose.'

unusual_words.definition: the quality of being plain or uncomplicated; guilelessness
unusual_words.context: Paired with 'naïve' to comment on human nature.

unusual_words.definition: property or money brought by a bride to her husband on marriage
unusual_words.context: Adelaïda's dowry is discussed as part of the marriage arrangement.

unusual_words.definition: divided and distributed
unusual_words.context: Her family 'apportioned the runaway bride her dowry.'

unusual_words.definition: a legal transfer of property; a document effecting transfer
unusual_words.context: Fyodor tried to transfer the village and town house by 'deed of conveyance.'

unusual_words.definition: quick to become angry
unusual_words.context: Describes Adelaïda as 'hot-tempered, bold, dark-browed.'

unusual_words.definition: having dark eyebrows or a stern aspect
unusual_words.context: Part of Adelaïda's physical description: 'dark-browed.'

unusual_words.definition: restless or short of temper under delay
unusual_words.context: Used in describing Adelaïda's temperament.

unusual_words.definition: worthy of attention; striking
unusual_words.context: Used in 'remarkable physical strength' describing Adelaïda.

unusual_words.definition: relating to the body
unusual_words.context: Appears in 'remarkable physical strength.'

unusual_words.definition: the quality or state of being strong
unusual_words.context: Used to emphasize Adelaïda's physical power.

unusual_words.definition: lacking the basic necessities of life; poor
unusual_words.context: Describes the 'destitute divinity student' who eloped with Adelaïda.

unusual_words.definition: the study of religion and theology; here, a divinity student
unusual_words.context: The eloper is called a 'divinity student.'

unusual_words.definition: a person studying at an educational institution
unusual_words.context: Used for the divinity student who ran away with Adelaïda.

unusual_words.definition: a group of women associated with one man; used figuratively
unusual_words.context: Fyodor 'introduced a regular harem into the house.'

unusual_words.definition: wild, unrestrained parties or excesses
unusual_words.context: Talks of 'orgies of drunkenness' in Fyodor's household.

unusual_words.definition: the state of being intoxicated with alcohol
unusual_words.context: Fyodor abandons himself to 'orgies of drunkenness.'

unusual_words.definition: excessive indulgence in sensual pleasures
unusual_words.context: The house is turned 'into a sink of debauchery.'

unusual_words.definition: deserving or inviting derision; absurd
unusual_words.context: He plays the 'ridiculous part' of the injured husband.

unusual_words.definition: a clown or foolish person who amuses others
unusual_words.context: Fyodor is repeatedly called a 'buffoon.'

unusual_words.definition: those who mock or jeer
unusual_words.context: The 'scoffers' mock Fyodor's ostentatious grief.

unusual_words.definition: persistent or urgent solicitation; troublesome persistence
unusual_words.context: His 'shameless importunity' in seeking his wife's property is criticized.

unusual_words.definition: feigned or made up; not genuine
unusual_words.context: He 'pretended to be unaware of his ludicrous position.'

unusual_words.definition: moved quickly on foot; rushed
unusual_words.context: He 'ran out into the street' when the news came of Adelaïda's death.

unusual_words.definition: Saint Petersburg; major Russian city
unusual_words.context: Adelaïda and the divinity student are said to have been in 'Petersburg.'

unusual_words.definition: a top-floor or attic room, often small and squalid
unusual_words.context: Adelaïda reportedly died 'in a garret' in Petersburg.

unusual_words.definition: an infectious disease causing fever and rash
unusual_words.context: One account says she died of 'typhus.'

unusual_words.definition: suffering from extreme hunger leading to death
unusual_words.context: Another version says she died of 'starvation.'

unusual_words.definition: cried; shed tears
unusual_words.context: Some say Fyodor 'wept without restraint like a little child.'

unusual_words.definition: a person employed in domestic duties
unusual_words.context: A 'faithful servant of the family, Grigory,' cared for Mitya.

unusual_words.definition: proper name of the faithful servant
unusual_words.context: Grigory 'took the three-year-old Mitya into his care.'

unusual_words.definition: the mother of one's father or mother
unusual_words.context: Mitya's 'widow, Mitya’s grandmother,' had moved to Moscow and was ill.

unusual_words.definition: capital city of Russia
unusual_words.context: Mitya's grandmother had 'moved to Moscow.'

unusual_words.definition: a child of one's aunt or uncle; relative
unusual_words.context: A 'cousin of Mitya’s mother, Pyotr Alexandrovitch Miüsov,' returns from Paris.

unusual_words.definition: proper name (Peter)
unusual_words.context: Pyotr Alexandrovitch Miüsov is introduced as the cousin.

unusual_words.definition: patronymic indicating 'son of Alexander'
unusual_words.context: Part of Pyotr's full Russian name 'Pyotr Alexandrovitch Miüsov.'

unusual_words.definition: family surname (with diacritic as in text)
unusual_words.context: Adelaïda's family name, the Miüsovs, appears several times.

unusual_words.definition: capital of France
unusual_words.context: Pyotr 'returned from Paris.'

unusual_words.definition: supporter of liberal political ideas
unusual_words.context: Pyotr 'became a Liberal of the type common in the forties and fifties.'

unusual_words.definition: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon, French political thinker (referenced)
unusual_words.context: Pyotr is said to have 'known Proudhon' personally.

unusual_words.definition: Mikhail Bakunin, Russian revolutionary (referenced)
unusual_words.context: Pyotr is also said to have 'known Bakunin personally.'

unusual_words.definition: the second month of the year
unusual_words.context: Reference to 'the Paris Revolution of February 1848.'

unusual_words.definition: a forcible overthrow of a government or social order
unusual_words.context: Mentions 'the Paris Revolution of February 1848.'

unusual_words.definition: becoming weaker or less vigorous
unusual_words.context: Pyotr 'in his declining years' liked to recount past events.

unusual_words.definition: plural of year; long periods of time
unusual_words.context: Used in reference to Pyotr's long life after his Paris sojourn.

unusual_words.definition: an area or region marked by administrative boundaries
unusual_words.context: Fyodor Pavlovitch was 'well known in our district.'

unusual_words.definition: an area of land with a large house, often owned by gentry

unusual_words.definition: to eat the principal meal; to take dinner
unusual_words.context: Fyodor 'ran to dine at other men's tables.'

unusual_words.definition: furniture at which people eat; used metonymically for hospitality
unusual_words.context: He dined at 'other men's tables.'

unusual_words.definition: attached oneself to; clung to
unusual_words.context: He 'fastened on them as a toady.'

unusual_words.definition: a profession or course of action pursued over time
unusual_words.context: He was 'passionately anxious to make a career in one way or another.'

unusual_words.definition: expressing dissatisfaction or annoyance
unusual_words.context: He spent time 'complaining tearfully' to others about his wife.

unusual_words.definition: ornamentation or exaggerations added to a story
unusual_words.context: He 'paraded his woes with embellishments.'

unusual_words.definition: harmed or offended; here used figuratively
unusual_words.context: He assumed the 'ridiculous part of the injured husband.'

unusual_words.definition: drops of clear salty liquid from the eyes, here indicating weeping
unusual_words.context: His 'tears and complaints' are described as part of his performance.

unusual_words.definition: to display ostentatiously
unusual_words.context: He 'paraded his woes' publicly.

unusual_words.definition: great sorrow or distress
unusual_words.context: He paraded 'his woes with embellishments.'

unusual_words.definition: to give pleasure or satisfaction to
unusual_words.context: What seemed to 'gratify him and flatter his self-love most' was playing the injured husband.

unusual_words.definition: to praise excessively, often insincerely
unusual_words.context: His self-love was 'flattered' by public sympathy.

unusual_words.definition: to act a role or perform theatrically
unusual_words.context: He 'played the ridiculous part' of the injured man.

unusual_words.definition: a small settlement in a rural area
unusual_words.context: Mentions 'the little village' included in Adelaïda's dowry.

unusual_words.definition: a populated area larger than a village
unusual_words.context: Refers to the 'rather fine town house' that was part of her dowry.

unusual_words.definition: held in memory; recalled
unusual_words.context: Fyodor Pavlovitch is 'still remembered among us' due to his death.

unusual_words.definition: the end of life
unusual_words.context: Reference to Adelaïda's 'death in Petersburg.'

unusual_words.definition: the act of being freed from something oppressive
unusual_words.context: Fyodor's exclamation 'now lettest Thou Thy servant depart in peace' implies release from marriage burdens.
